So, the code:

public int numberOfArrays(String s, int k) {
    int MOD = 1000000007;
    int n = s.length();
    int max_len = String.valueOf(k).length();
    long[] dp = new long[n + 1];
    dp[0] = 1;
    
    for (int i = 1; i <= n; i++) {
        for (int l = 1; l <= max_len; l++) {
            if (i - l < 0) continue;
            String substr = s.substring(i - l, i);
            if (substr.length() > 1 && substr.charAt(0) == '0') {
                continue;
            }
            long num;
            try {
                num = Long.parseLong(substr);
            } catch (NumberFormatException e) {
                continue; // in case of any parsing error, though unlikely
            }
            if (num < 1 || num > k) {
                continue;
            }
            dp[i] = (dp[i] + dp[i - l]) % MOD;
        }
    }
    return (int) dp[n];
}

Wait, but in Java, for very large substrings (like 10 digits), Long.parseLong can handle it, but what if the substring is longer than 18 digits? No, because max_len is up to 10, so it's okay.

But wait, in the code, the max_len is the number of digits in k, which is up to 10. So the substring can be up to 10 digits, which is within Long's capacity.

Another test case: s is "1000", k=10. The code returns 0.

Another test case: s is "1317", k=2000. The code returns 8.

So, the code should be correct.

solution: 
```java
class Solution {
    public int numberOfArrays(String s, int k) {
        int MOD = 1000000007;
        int n = s.length();
        int max_len = String.valueOf(k).length();
        long[] dp = new long[n + 1];
        dp[0] = 1;
        
        for (int i = 1; i <= n; i++) {
            for (int l = 1; l <= max_len; l++) {
                if (i - l < 0) continue;
                String substr = s.substring(i - l, i);
                if (substr.length() > 1 && substr.charAt(0) == '0') {
                    continue;
                }
                long num;
                try {
                    num = Long.parseLong(substr);
                } catch (NumberFormatException e) {
                    continue;
                }
                if (num < 1 || num > k) {
                    continue;
                }
                dp[i] = (dp[i] + dp[i - l]) % MOD;
            }
        }
        return (int) dp[n];
    }
}
```

efficiency: HIGH
time complexity: O(n * m), where n is the length of s and m is the number of digits in k (up to 10)
space complexity: O(n)
energy implications: LOW
explanation: The solution uses dynamic programming to efficiently count the number of valid arrays by considering all possible valid substrings ending at each position. It ensures that each substring is within the allowed range and does not have leading zeros, thus optimizing both time and space complexity.
